coredata.dc:title: A proof of concept study of preparing ultra bright silicon quantum dots based on synergistic effect of reductants
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Shiyao
Yue, Tong
Xiao, Xincai
Cheng, Han
Zhao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: The concept of synergistic effect has been widely applied into various scientific research areas, ranging from life science to material science, nevertheless it has been rarely adopted in the synthesis of quantum dots (QDs). This proof-of-concept study was the preparation of ultra-bright silicon QDs with a relative quantum yield of up to 84.92% based on the synergistic effect of two reductants, whilst silane coupling agent as the silicon source. Compared with the conventional one-reductant strategy, our as-prepared silicon QDs exhibit greatly enhanced quantum yields with even tunable emission wavelength, meanwhile, inheriting other merits including good pH, salt tolerance, small particle size and amino-rich surface, which is conducive to biological applications. The highly efficient and stable photoluminescence of silicon QDs could definitely facilitate their prospective applications as optical materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Synergistic effect
Reductant
Synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SiQDs prepared by using single reductant
The synergistic effect of Na-citrate with other reductants
The optimization of experiment factors for reductant pair of Na-Citrate and thiourea
The optimization of experimental factors for reductant pair of CA and H2C2O4
The impacts of reaction time and temperature
Fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption
The mechanism of synergistic effect of reductants

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2016CFB615 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the “Central Universities”, South Central University for Nationalities ( CZY15020 , CZW15017 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: QYs of SiQDs synthesized with single reductant (Na-Citrate) and double reductants (Na-Citrate with other reductants).

PL spectra of SiQDs synthesized (a) with different molar ratios of reductants (Na-Citrate and thiourea) to silicon source DAMO, and (b) with different molar ratios of Na-Citrate to thiourea. The insets show the corresponding QYs.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of SiQDs synthesized with single reductant of Na-Citrate, and double reductants of Na-Citrate and thiourea.
QYs of SiQDs synthesized with double reductants of CA and other reductants.
PL spectra of SiQDs synthesized with CA and Na2C2O4 (a) at different reaction temperatures and (b) at different reaction times. The insets displayed the corresponding QYs.
PL spectra of SiQDs synthesized with CA and Na2C2O4 at different pH values. The inset shows the corresponding QYs.
HRTEM image of prepared SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4).
IR spectra of SiQDs prepared by single reductant and two reductants. Spectra are offset for clarity.
EDS pattern of prepared SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4). Table in the inset presents the elemental ratios.
UV–vis absorption spectra of SiQDs synthesized with CA and Na2C2O4 alone or the mixture of CA and Na2C2O4.
Schematic illustration of SiQDs synthesized with reductant pair of CA and Na2C2O4.
QYs of SiQDs synthesized with different reductants.
QYs of SiQDs synthesized with reductant pairs.

head.ce:title: A proof of concept study of preparing ultra bright silicon quantum dots based on synergistic effect of reductants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central University for Nationalities), Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central University for Nationalities)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The concept of synergistic effect has been widely applied into various scientific research areas, ranging from life science to material science, nevertheless it has been rarely adopted in the synthesis of quantum dots (QDs). This proof-of-concept study was the preparation of ultra-bright silicon QDs with a relative quantum yield of up to 84.92% based on the synergistic effect of two reductants, whilst silane coupling agent as the silicon source. Compared with the conventional one-reductant strategy, our as-prepared silicon QDs exhibit greatly enhanced quantum yields with even tunable emission wavelength, meanwhile, inheriting other merits including good pH, salt tolerance, small particle size and amino-rich surface, which is conducive to biological applications. The highly efficient and stable photoluminescence of silicon QDs could definitely facilitate their prospective applications as optical materials.

ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic effect is an effect arising between two or more subsystems that produces an effect greater than the sum of their individual effects (1 + 1 > 2). It is ubiquitous in natural and social sciences, and has been widely applied into the life science [1–3], health care [4–12], chemistry and chemical engineering [13–18] and material science [19–36], etc. The discovery of synergistic effect of cytokines and adhesion-dependent signals that leads to long-lived plasma cell is important for our understanding of the mechanism regulating the homeostasis of plasma cells, which can guide the treatment of allergy and autoimmune diseases [1]. The synergistic effect of silver nanoparticles in combination with selected broad spectrum antibiotics against the tested bacteria determined strong growth inhibitory activity, providing an possible emerging strategy to combat disease resistant pathogens in future [4]. Beyond the significance of synergistic effect in the life science and health care, it has been extensively adopted in chemical synthesis for the purpose of reducing reaction temperature, reaction time, possibility of side reaction, enhancing the conversion rate and improving the quality of products. For example, the synthesis of acrylates could be accelerated through an efficacious photoinduced living polymerization due to the synergistic catalysis of CuBr2 and tertiary amine. The near-quantitative monomer conversion (> 95%) is obtained within 80 min (generally take 12–32 h), yielding poly(acrylates) with dispersities as low as 1.05 and excellent end group fidelity (> 99%) [13]. Photocatalysts of g-C3N4/ TiO2 nanosheets which can degrade almost all RhB under simulated-sunlight irradiation within 50 min were synthesized by a facile biomimetic method. The significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity toward organic pollutant degradation (Rhodamine B) benefited from the synergistic effect of large surface area of nanosheets and small size of TiO2 nanoparticle [14]. In spite of the vital function of synergistic effect in the chemical synthesis, this concept has been rarely practiced in the synthesis of quantum dots (QDs). It is worth noting that the N-S [20–22], N-P [23] and N-B [23–25] co-doped carbon dots (CDs) have already demonstrated improved photochemical activity, such as high quantum yields (QYs) [20], owing to the synergistic effect of multiple elements, thereby expending their potential application areas (eg. photocatalysis [25]). This is indicative of that the synergistic effect has immense potential to improve the character of the QDs.

The silicon QDs (SiQDs) have aroused great attention thanks to their non-toxicity, richness in raw materials and excellent biocompatibility. Recently, a newly developed bottom-to-top method for preparing SiQDs in aqueous phase with silane coupling agent as silicon source and reductant as the catalysis agent becomes popular. Several approaches including microwave route [28–30], hydrothermal route [31–34] and ultraviolet radiation route [35] have been utilized in SiQDs preparation. Meanwhile, multiple silane coupling agents, such as (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) [28,31,32,35], 3-amino-propyltriethoxysilane (APTES) [29,31,36], urea propyl triethoxysilane (UPTES) [33], 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) [30], and N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) [30,34] have been successfully used to prepare SiQDs. The difference of reductants in their deoxidization ability, mechanism of action, interaction speed, solubility, and reaction product, would greatly impact the optical properties of prepared SiQDs. The blue SiQDs (λem = 445 nm) with super-high QY (82.4%) has been successfully prepared using DAMO as the silicon source and sodium citrate (Na-Citrate) as the reductant [30]. However, with the same silicon source (DAMO), replacing the reductant of Na-Citrate to catechol resulted in green SiQDs (λem = 512 nm) with quite low QY (7.1%) [34]. The green SiQDs can also be achieved by using APTES as silicon source and ascorbate sodium (AS) as the reductant, whilst the maximum emission wavelength was at 530 nm with QY of 21% [36]. Though Na-Citrate is the most commonly used reductant, and the only one that could produce QDs with super-high QY, it can only prepare blue-fluorescence SiQDs. On the other hand, though other reductants could generate QDs with different fluorescence emission wavelengths, the QYs of the products are rather low. The synergistic effect of different kinds of reductants was thus expected to break the bottleneck, and produce SiQDs with both tunable emission wavelength and high QYs.

Utilizing only single reductant in the preparation is not conductive to the improvement of the properties of QDs, which also confines the rational design of high-quality SiQDs under the theoretical guidance. Therefore, in this work, with DAMO as the silicon source, the synergistic effect of reductants in the preparation of SiQDs was systematically explored for the first time. Two pairs of reductant combination were successfully screened out. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the SiQDs with super-high QY (84.92%) have been prepared. Besides, the optical properties, pH tolerance and salt tolerance of the prepared SiQDs with both single-reductant and double-reductant approaches have been compared. What’s more, the possible mechanism of synergistic effect of reductants has been proposed. This work demonstrates the broad application prospect of incorporating the concept of synergistic effect into the synthesis of QDs. It is thus expected that the synergistic effect could attract more attention from researches, and be fully utilized in the synthesis of SiQDs as well as other types of QDs in the future.

DAMO (95.0%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., Na-Citrate (99.0%), citric acid monohydrate (CA) (≥ 99.5%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) (≥ 96.0%), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) (≥ 99.8%), oxalic acid dihydrate (H2C2O4) (≥ 99.5%), sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3) (≥ 97.0%), urea (≥ 99.0%), thiourea (≥ 99.0%), hydrazine hydrate 85% (HHA) (≥ 85.0%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Catechol (≥ 99.0%) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). L-Cysteine (L-Cys) (> 99.9%), Glycine (Gly) (≥ 99%) were purchased from Sigma Corporation (USA). All chemicals used were of analytical grade or of the highest purity available. All solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (Millipore) as the solvent.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda-35 UV/ Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Company). Fluorescence spectra were acquired with a LS55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer Company). All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions. The pH was monitored by a PHSJ-3F pH meter (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instrument Company). The MDS-6 G microwave chemical reactor (Shanghai SINEO Microwave Chemistry Technology Company) was used to synthesize SiQDs. TEM sample was prepared by dropping an aqueous SiQDs solution onto Agar carbon-coated copper grids (400 meshes) with the excess solvent evaporated. TEM image was obtained with a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (Japen Electron Optics Laboratory Company). EDS patterns were captured using an FEI Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (FEI Company). Fourier transform infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific).

The SiQDs were prepared through a microwave-assisted method with DAMO as the silicon source and Na-Citrate, CA, NaBH4, Na2C2O4, H2C2O4, Na2SO3, urea, thiourea, HHA, L-Cys, catechol and Gly as the reductants. In a typical preparation procedure, Na-Citrate (0.0621 g, 0.2112 mmol), thiourea (0.0161 g, 0.2112 mmol) and 0.2 ml DAMO (0.88 mmol) were mixed in 20 ml of water under stirring, followed by deaerating with high purity nitrogen gas for 20 min. The precursor solution was adjusted to specified pH value. Finally, the precursor solution was loaded in the microwave oven at 200 °C for 15 min. For other SiQDs, similar procedures were employed except for replacing the reactants of Na-Citrate and thiourea with single or combination of other reactants including CA, NaBH4, Na2C2O4, H2C2O4, Na2SO3, urea, HHA, L-Cys, catechol and Gly The molar ratio of DAMO: reductant was 1: 0.48, the concentration of DAMO was 44 mM, the reaction temperature was 200 °C and reaction time was 15 min, unless otherwise specified.

The QYs of SiQDs were calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensities and absorbance values of the samples (exited at 360 nm), using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 aqueous solution (refractive index (η) of 1.33) as the standard (QY = 54%) [37,38]. All samples dissolved in water (η = 1.33) had absorbance less than 0.1 at 360 nm. The relative QYs can be calculated using the below equation:
Φ
X
=
Φ
ST
(
Grad
X
/
Grad
ST
)
(
η
X
2
/
η
ST
2
)
where Φ is the QY, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent; ST denotes the standard and X denotes the sample. To obtain more reliable results, a series of solutions of SiQDs and referenced fluorescence dye with low absorbance values (0–0.1) at 360 nm were prepared for QYs calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SiQDs prepared by using single reductant
ce:section.ce:para: With DAMO as the silicon source, the impacts of various commonly used reductants upon the optical properties of prepared SiQDs have been investigated, including the organic reductants (Na-Citrate, CA, thiourea, urea, H2C2O4, Na2C2O4, HHA, L-Cys, Gly), and inorganic reductants (Na2SO3, NaBH4). As shown in Tables 1 and S1, fixing the molar ratio of DAMO to the reductant at 1:0.48, the qualities of SiQDs prepared by using different reductants vary greatly. Only reductant Na-Citrate could produce SiQDs with high QY of 73.27% (Fig. S1b), while SiQDs prepared using other reductants generate relatively low QYs (less than 25%), which might be attributed to the different deoxidization capacities of reductants. It worth nothing that, the QY of SiQDs prepared with reductant Na-Citrate (73.27%) was three times higher than that of SiQDs prepared with reductant CA (24.20%). From the perspective of chemical component, Na-Citrate is the salt of CA, indicating the similar deoxidization capacities. Therefore, it is thus inferred that the pH value of the precursor solution might also impact the quality of the SiQDs. Because the pH value of Na-Citrate precursor solution is 10.39, while that is 7.87 for CA precursor solution. In order to verify the assumption, the pH value of CA precursor solution was adjusted to 10.93 using 1 mol/L NaOH, with other experimental factors fixed. To our delight, the QY of prepared SiQDs enhanced to 42.23%, which proved the pivotal effect of pH value on the QY of as-prepared SiQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic effect of Na-citrate with other reductants

ce:section.ce:para: Na-Citrate is not only the most commonly used but also the only reductant that can produce SiQDs with high QY, the synergistic effect between Na-Citrate and another reductant was thus firstly explored. With other experimental factors unaltered, fixing the molar ratio of DAMO to the total amount of reductants and that of Na-Citrate to another reductant at 1:0.48 and 1:1, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the QYs of most SiQDs prepared by using Na-Citrate-contained double reductants did not increase but greatly decreased instead. However, the combination of Na-Citrate and thiourea greatly enhanced the QY from 73.27% to 84.92%, suggesting the feasibility of improving the quality of SiQDs via making full use of the synergistic effect of reductants. Importantly, it is observed that the synergistic effect could also impact the fluorescence emission wavelengths of prepared SiQDs. For example, the maximum emission wavelengths of the SiQDs prepared with single reductant Na-Citrate and catechol are at 452 nm and 534 nm, respectively. Whereas, the maximum emission wavelength of SiQDs prepared with double reductants (Na-Citrate/ catechol) lies in the middle, at 495 nm, with QY (31.44%) four times higher than that of QDs prepared with catechol alone (7.85%). As shown in Fig. S2, SiQDs (Na-Citrate) exhibit blue fluorescence while SiQDs (Na-Citrate/ catechol) emit green fluorescence. Simply through combining two types of reductants in the synthesis process could attain both goals of improving QYs and tuning the emission wavelengths, validating the significance of synergistic effect in the controllable synthesis of QDs. Since this paper mainly focuses on how to enhance the QYs of SiQDs, the detailed information regarding the emission peak shifts would be skipped in the following discussion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of experiment factors for reductant pair of Na-Citrate and thiourea
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain SiQDs with super-high QYs, the following experimental factors have been systematically optimized, including the total amount of reductants, the molar ratio of Na-Citrate to thiourea, the reaction temperature and time.

With other factors fixed, the effect of molar ratio of reductants to silicon source DAMO was firstly investigated, which was defined as A (A = MNa-Citrate+thiourea/ MDAMO). As shown in Fig. 2a, when A increases from 0.2 to 0.7 with the molar ratio of Na-Citrate to thiourea fixed at 1: 1, the QYs of prepared SiQDs first enhances and then decreases. It is thus concluded that both insufficiency and excessiveness of reductants are unbeneficial to the synthesis of high quality SiQDs. During the formation of SiQDs, the small molecules of silanes can form crystal nucleus through oxidation-reduction reaction with the presence of reductants [28]. Therefore, the insufficient amount of reductant would hinder the formation of crystal nucleus. On the other hand, when the total amount of reductants is excessive, the silicon source would first interact with the strong reductant Na-Citrate, rendering less participation of thiourea (rich in N and S elements) in the interaction and thus unbeneficial to the formation of N/ S-codoped SiQDs. The doping of N and S elements is proven to be effective in improving the QYs of the prepared QDs [20]. Therefore, the optimal A was set at 0.48.

Subsequently, with A fixed at 0.48, the molar ratio of Na-Citrate to thiourea was studied. As demonstrated in Fig. 2b, the molar ratio of two deoxidizers sets limited impact upon the QYs with the fluctuation at only 19.20%. Although the molar ratio of Na-Citrate to thiourea displayed less impacts on the QYs of SiQDs prepared via double-reductant strategy, it does significantly affect the QYs of SiQDs prepared under extreme molar ratio conditions. As mentioned above, the QY of SiQDs prepared with double reductants (Na-Citrate and thiourea) reaches 84.92%, whilst the SiQDs prepared with single reductant of Na-Citrate and thiourea are 73.27% and 2.11%, respectively. Interestingly, even when thiourea works as the main content of the double reductants (the molar ratio of Na-Citrate to thiourea is 0.14), the QY was still high (69.71%).

With the molar ratio of DAMO to Na-Citrate and thiourea setting at 1:0.24:0.24, the optimized reaction temperature and reaction time were found to be 200 °C and 15 min, respectively. The detailed discussion were presented in the supporting information. Thus, the optimized condition was found out, the molar ratio of DAMO to Na-Citrate and thiourea is 1:0.24:0.24, the temperature is 200 °C, and the reaction time is 15 min. Under the optimized condition, the SiQDs (λem = 452 nm) with QY up to 84.92% (Fig. S1c) was successfully prepared with narrow full-width-at-half-maxima (FWHM = 69 nm).

Apart from its positive effect on the QY and emission wavelength of SiQDs, the synergistic effect of Na-Citrate and thiourea is also in favor of the improvement of SiQDs productivity. As shown in Fig. 3, the absorbance of SiQDs prepared with Na-Citrate and thiourea is four times higher than that of SiQDs prepared with Na-Citrate alone at the maximum absorption wavelength (360 nm). Since the silicon source DAMO and the reductants Na-Citrate/ thiourea exhibit no absorption peak at 300–400 nm (Fig. S4), the absorbance of the first exciton absorption can be used to calculate the concentration of prepared SiQDs according to the Lambert-Bill Law. Combined with the fact that the amounts of silicon source and reductants were fixed, the productivity of the SiQDs prepared with double reductants was thus reckoned to be much higher than that prepared with single reductant. To our delight, the synergistic effect of double reductants could enhance the quality of the product as well as the productivity of the synthesis, which is beneficial to the mass production of SiQDs and to meet the requirements of energy saving and high efficiency in industrial production.

The proof of positive synergistic effect of Na-Citrate and thiourea on the quality of SiQDs drive us to explore the other possible synergistic reductant pairs. As shown in Tables 2 and S2, fixing the molar ratio of DAMO to reductant 1 and reductant 2 at 1:0.24:0.24, the QYs of all SiQDs prepared with reductant pairs exhibit modest enhancement compared with those prepared by single reductant. For instance, the QYs of SiQDs prepared with single reductant of Na2SO3 and NaBH4 are only 12.75% and 11.88% (Table 1), respectively. But the QY enhanced to 30.70% with using double reductants of Na2SO3 and NaBH4 (Table 2). Specially, the combination of CA and Na2C2O4 causes the most significant enhancement of QYs. The possible combinations of CA with another reductant were thus tested. As shown in Fig. 4, the optimum reductant pair of CA and Na2C2O4 was screened out based on the QYs of the products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of experimental factors for reductant pair of CA and H2C2O4
The impacts of reaction time and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: With the molar ratio of DAMO to CA and Na2C2O4 at 1: 0.1125: 0.0375 (the detailed optimization of molar ratio of reactants was discussed in the supporting information), the impacts of the reaction time and temperature were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, in the range of 170–200 °C, the QYs of prepared SiQDs enhances with the increase of reaction temperature. However, the over-high temperature renders decreased QYs. The high temperature is beneficial to the fast formation of QDs core, while over-high temperature, the polymerization of silane and the serious carbonization of external alkyl groups would lead to the decrease of fluorescence intensity [39]. Thus, the optimal temperature was set at 200 °C.

However, the reaction time sets lesser impact upon the QYs of prepared SiQDs, and the highest QY occurs in 10 min (Fig. 5b). It’s worth noting that the QY already reaches 73.69% in the first 5 min, which is 91.99% of the best QY (80.10%). Comparatively, the optimal reaction time for SiQDs prepared with reductant pair of Na-Citrate and thiourea is longer (t = 15 min), and the QY (61.23%) only reaches 72.10% of the best QY (84.92%) in the first 5 min. Therefore, the combination of CA and Na2C2O4 is better option for SiQDs mass preparation, because of the shortened reaction time and thus the lesser energy consumption.

With other experimental factors fixed, the pH value of the precursor solution containing silicon source (the pH of the original solution at 10), CA and Na2C2O4 was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 6, the pH value greatly impact the QY of prepared SiQDs. The QY gradually enhances with the increase of pH, and reaches the highest at pH of 10. It is inferred that the amino in silane is prone to be protonated and thus expels each other in the acidic condition. It is unbeneficial to the aggregation of silane and thus the preparation of SiQDs. Therefore, the decrease of acidity of the solution would easily render the fluorescence enhancement. On the other hand, in the strong alkali environment (pH > 10), the silane will firstly hydrolyzed into silanol [35],which is prone to be deprotonated and expels each other, preventing the aggregation of silane. It declines the reaction efficiency during the formation of the network-structured Si-O-Si core and consequently impedes the formation of fluorescent SiQDs [30]. Thence, we didn’t adjust the pH of the precursor solution.

Besides, the tolerance to environment interference including pH and salt was investigated. Moreover, the comparison between the SiQDs prepared under the optimized condition and the SiQDs prepared with single Na-Citrate was made. Under the extremely acidic condition (2 < pH < 3), the PL intensities of both types of QDs are relatively low. But both remain stable in a wide range of pH from 4 to 14 (Fig. S6a, b). As shown in Fig. S6c, d, the PL intensities of both QDs are quite stable in the presence of NaCl (0–2 mol/L), showing the excellent salt tolerance of both QDs. The SiQDs prepared with reductant pair of CA and Na2C2O4 exhibit greatly enhanced QYs and inherit good pH and salt tolerance, which renders them good candidate in the biochemical detection and biological imagining applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S7 demonstrates the PL spectra and the UV–vis absorption spectra of SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) and SiQDs (NA-Citrate/ thiourea) prepared under optimal condition. Two kinds of SiQDs emit bright blue fluorescence under the illumination of UV (365 nm) light (inset of Fig. S7). Both have absorption peaks at 360 nm. The optimum excitation wavelength and fluorescence emission wavelength of them are both 360 nm and 452 nm respectively. With quinoline sulfate as the reference(QY = 54%), the QYs of SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) and SiQDs (NA-Citrate/ thiourea) reach 80.10% and 84.92% respectively (The specific calculation process is shown in Fig. S1).

TEM has been widely used to study the morphology of SiQDs. Fig. 7 displays the HRTEM image of prepared SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4). It is quite evident that SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) is close to spherical with good dispersion and is uniform with an average diameter as 4.1 nm. In the meantime, the average diameter of SiQDs (Na-Citrate/ thiourea) and SiQDs (Na-Citrate) are also measured at 4.7 nm and 4.3 nm respectively (Fig. S8). Therefore, the particle sizes of three SiQDs are basically no difference.

IR was utilized to study the surface groups of SiQDs. Fig. 8 shows IR spectra of SiQDs prepared by single reductant and double reductants, the spectra of SiQDs (Na-Citrate) and SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) are basically similar, but the spectra of SiQDs (Na-Citrate/ thiourea) is slightly different. The broad absorption peaks at 3400 cm−1 is due to the N-H stretching vibration, the absorbance peaks at 2930 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H bending vibration, the absorption peaks at 1580 cm−1 stands for the N-H bending vibration. The above peaks are possessed by all three SiQDs. The Si-O-Si stretching vibration of SiQDs (Na-Citrate) and SiQDs(CA/ Na2C2O4) are located at 1130 cm−1and 1030 cm−1, however, the Si-O-Si of SiQDs (Na-Citrate/ thiourea) lies in 1010 cm−1. Compared to the other two SiQDs, the one more absorption peaks of SiQDs (Na-Citrate/ thiourea) at 1450 cm−1 is due to the N-C˭S stretching vibration, resulting from thiourea in the raw materials.

EDS was used to explore the elemental composition of SiQDs. We measured EDS patterns of SiQDs (Na-Citrate) and SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) as a double reductants representative. As shown in Fig. 9, SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) include Si, O and N of 12.74, 22.12, 13.91% wt concentration, respectively. Then, SiQDs (Na-Citrate) contain Si, O and N of 12.83%, 27.11%, and 12.13% wt concentration, respectively (Fig. S9). The concentration of N in SiQDs synthesized by double reductants is higher than the SiQDs synthesized by single reductant. However, N elements have a positive effect on the QYs [40], which may account for the high QYs of SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of synergistic effect of reductants
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic effect has been proven to be effective in improving the QYs of prepared SiQDs, but it is quite hard to dig out the possible mechanism. Because the types of available reductants vary, the reaction process is complex, and the theoretical foundation from literatures is weak. Yu et al. have reported the preparation of nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) and N/ sulfur codoped CDs (N/ S-CDs) with QY of the former at 16.9% and the QY of the latter at 73%. They regarded the possible mechanism was the doping of N atoms could introduce the CDs a new kind of surface state, whose density could be increased dramatically by the codoped S atoms, which enhanced the fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs [41]. Furthermore, You et al. discovered the surface state band structure of N/ S-CDs was more uniform and has less defects than that of N-CDs. They deduced that the doping of S atoms onto N-CDs could effectively enhance the ratio of sp2/sp3 of carbon core, and more isolated sp2 conjugated carbon clusters could effectively enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs [42]. Chen et al. also prepared N-CDs and N,S-CDs with QYs at 2.6% and 39.7% respectively. They proposed that S atoms promoted the strong photoluminescence of CDs [43]. In summary, the existence of S elements is proven to be effective in enhance the QYs of CDs. Though no relevant literatures have reported this phenomena in the synthesis of SiQDs, but the fluorescence mechanism of SiQDs is similar to that of CDs [30]. Wu et al. proposed that the existence of NH2 changed the electronic band gap on the surface of SiQDs and thus enhanced the QYs and UV–Vis absorbance of SiQDs [30], and the presence of thiourea also improved the QYs and UV–Vis absorbance (Fig. 3). Therefore, we speculate that the doping of S elements might change the electronic band gap on the surface of SiQDs, and thus enhanced the QYs. As shown in Figs. 8 and S10, N and S elements have been successfully doped into SiQDs. For the synergistic effect of Na-Citrate/ thiourea pair, it is inferred that the abundant N and S elements in thiourea are beneficial to the formation of high quality N/ S-codoped SiQDs [20].

With respect to the synergistic effect of CA/ Na2C2O4 pair, two main reasons probably contribute to the enhanced PL intensity. On the one hand, the increased pH value might lead to the increased PL intensity. Since the QY of SiQDs is greatly affected by the pH value of the precursor solution, whilst CA is acidic, which is disadvantageous to the formation of SiQDs with high quality. However, the addition of Na2C2O4 (alkaline) would effectively enhance the pH value. When the molar ratio of CA to Na2C2O4 is in the range of 0.5–6 (Fig. S5), the pH value of the precursor solution maintains within 9.8–10.2, quite close to the optimum pH of 10. On the other hand, the reduced surface defect of QDs caused by the synergistic effect of CA/ Na2C2O4 pair might be another reason results in the enhancement of the QYs. As shown in Fig. 10, the maximum absorption of the SiQDs prepared with CA alone is much higher than that of QDs prepared with CA/ Na2C2O4 pair, which means a large amount of nanoparticles already formed with CA as the only reductant. However, the QY of SiQDs prepared with single reductant CA is only 24.20%. This probably because there are more surface defects on the SiQDs, and the high possibility of nonradioctive energy transition results in low PL intensity [44]. While the synergistic effect between CA and Na2C2O4 renders more perfect surface structure, and thus enhances the PL intensity. The synergistic effect is shown in Scheme 1.

For the present, many research teams regarded that the fluorescence of SiQDs originated from the quantum confinement effect [28,30,45], and so do we. Compared with SiQDs prepared by single reductant, the SiQDs prepared by double reductants exhibit enhanced QYs, which might because the co-doping of N and S elements enhanced the radiation efficiency of electron hole, and the reduction of surface defects also reduced the possibility of non-radiation. But it is believed that the detailed mechanism of synergistic effect is far more complex than we proposed. For example, with the amount of raw material fixed, the UV–Vis absorption of SiQDs (CA/ Na2C2O4) is obviously weaker than that of SiQDs (CA) (Fig. 10), but the absorption of SiQDs (Na-Citrate/ thiourea) is much stronger than that of SiQDs (Na-Citrate). More efforts will be put in the synthesis of SiQDs for further understanding of the mechanism and applications of the synergistic effect.

This paper reports the synthesis of SiQDs with super-high QYs through taking advantage of the synergistic effect of double reductants. The synergistic effect is proven to be effective in tuning the fluorescence emission wavelengths and further improving the QYs of prepared QDs. Na-Citrate is the most excellent reductant ever reported in literatures. The QYs of SiQDs prepared with single reductant of Na-Citrate reaches 73.27% under our experimental conditions, but the combination with thiourea could further enhance the QYs up to 84.92% with unaltered UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission peaks, narrow FWHM, small particle size and amino-rich surface. Moreover, the universality of synergistic effect in QDs preparation has been verified through screening other reductant pairs. Even the high QY (80.10%) can be achieved within 10 min through combining two types of low efficient reductant (CA and Na2C2O4), meantime, these SiQDs have good salt tolerance and pH stability. The possible mechanism of synergistic effect was briefly discussed. It is hoped that with the joint efforts of more researchers, the synergistic effect could play a more important role in improving the performance of QDs as well as guiding the synthesis of QDs from the theoretical perspective.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2016CFB615), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the “Central Universities”, South Central University for Nationalities (CZY15020, CZW15017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.04.006.
